title: Maximum Ice Cream Bars
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C++ proposal. solution.cpp is both the recommended fast + lean implementation and the speed extreme. The memory extreme is implemented in C; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: The required counting-sort workload is a dense frequency fill over n <= 100000 and a fixed price scan over R = 100000. C++ gives tight native loops, a compact stack int[100001] table, predictable integer arithmetic, and no managed or interpreted runtime overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It provides the best default/runtime balance for LeetCode: native loops, simple vector input, and a fixed contiguous frequency table.
Competitive for raw loops and selected for the memory-specific capped-domain variant, but it does not improve the fixed-table default beyond equivalent native code.
Native and memory-safe, but bounds checks and more verbose array setup bring no practical advantage for this tiny direct-addressing loop.
Compiled and simple, but slice bounds checks and runtime baseline are less attractive for top-percentile fixed-range scanning.
Primitive arrays are viable, but managed-runtime startup and GC baseline are unnecessary compared with C++'s compact stack storage.
The algorithm is loop-heavy over up to 100000 items and 100000 price slots, so interpreter or VM overhead is dominated for top-percentile runtime.

variants.approachJustification: Count every price in a fixed frequency table, then greedily consume price buckets from cheapest to most expensive. Bucket-level purchases avoid per-item subtraction for duplicates.

reasoningSummary: An exchange argument shows the optimal set is the longest affordable prefix of prices in ascending order. Because prices are bounded by 100000, counting sort represents that order with direct-addressed frequencies and consumes whole buckets greedily.
